answer:
**Allama Iqbal's "Shama-o-Parwana" (The Candle and the Moth)** appears as chapter 41 in *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*.[^1]

> [p-0d1630] The Candle and the Moth  
>   
> [p-21ea6d] O Candle, why for you does it hold love so vast?  
> Why is its restless soul upon your altar cast?  
>   
> [p-b7dff9] Your grace makes it mercurial, ever in sway;  
> Have you then taught it passion's sacred rite and way?  
>   
> [p-5a838f] It circumambulates your stage of splendor bright;  
> Is it then singed by your gaze's electric light?  
>   
> [p-21357c] In the anguish of death, does it find its soul's own ease?  
> In your devouring flame, is life that cannot cease?  
>   
> [p-4cfe14] In this world's house of grief, if your radiance were not seen,  
> This burning heart's own tree of hope would not be green.  
>   
> [p-d2c29e] To fall before you is its sacred prayer and rite;  
> Its tiny heart finds bliss in burning with delight.  
>   
> [p-4dcab6] It holds a passion for the Eternal Form Divine;  
> A little Moses, on a miniature Sinai.  
>   
> [p-366a1c] A simple moth, and such a thirst for light's display!  
> A tiny insect, and for light to yearn this way![^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 41 ("Shama-o-Parwana"), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/41-shama-o-parwana/translation/).
